12.2.label: Question 12.2
12.2.question:
Outline the reasons why industry data are unlikely to be as relevant as own data, for setting
assumptions for a long‐term health insurance product.

Solution: Industry experience is a ‘market average’, ie it is the average claims experience amongst all the
policyholders covered by those insurance companies that contribute to the pooled data.
One of the key problems is the much greater heterogeneity that exists between the experiences
of different insurers, even where they may be described as relating to the same product type.
 approach to underwriting and a:          policy conditions (eg different diseases allowed as a CI claim by different companies)
         claims management (eg different degree of effort made to rehabilitate IP claimants to get
         distribution method and channels (leading to different types of lives insured)
Note that it is policy conditions and claims management that make heterogeneity so much more
of a problem for morbidity data than for mortality data.
Furthermore, industry data are likely to be more out of date than own data.

12.3.label: Question 12.3
12.3.question: List the reasons why adjustments might be required to overseas data.

Possible reasons are:          different subsets of lives (relative to the population) take out insurance policies in the two
countries, which may be a consequence of different:
         different policy conditions (even if the class of lives is equivalent)
         the overseas population morbidity data may be compiled differently, using different
disease criteria or groupings, making comparison inappropriate
 claims management procedures m:          market practices, eg relating to claims disputes, may be different
         legislation / regulation, eg on rating and acceptance of policies, may be different
 the data may not relate to the:          the overseas industry data are likely to be very heterogeneous, so it is unlikely that any
single insurance company’s experience will reflect the industry average.

12.4.label: Question 12.4
12.4.scenario:
A health insurance company has written unit‐linked IP insurance contracts in its domestic market
   for many years, and has a significant share of that market. It now intends to start writing this
business in an overseas market. In that market, unit‐linked sickness contracts are rare, although
there is a substantial amount of without‐profits income protection insurance business written.
12.4.question:
Discuss the problems related to the main sources of data that the insurance company will face in
entering this market.

Solution: Morbidity experience and lapse rates – The higher the level of lapses, the greater the opportunity
for selective lapsing, where better risks leave, causing the morbidity experience for the business
as a whole to deteriorate.
Morbidity experience and claims expenses – Claims expenses need to be consistent with the
assumptions that determine claim volume.
Morbidity experience and investment return – Claim inception rates (and termination rates, where
applicable) will be affected by the general economic climate, which in turn will be related to the
investment return assumption.
New business volumes and fixed expenses – The loading for fixed expenses will reflect the
expected volumes of new business.
Business volumes and lapse / non‐renewal rates – The lapse and non‐renewal rates will also affect
volume.
Lapse / non‐renewal rates and investment return – Lapse / non‐renewal rates can be affected by
the general economic climate, which will be one of the major factors behind the investment
return assumption.
Investment return and inflation – The long‐term relationship between the rate of expense
inflation, benefit inflation and the rates of return on different types of assets must be consistent.
In addition to consistency between individual assumptions, the basis should normally be
consistent between related products. In fact, the basis of some closely related product might
form the start point for any new product’s basis. For example, the basis for a new stand‐alone CI
product might take as a start point the insurer’s current accelerated CI product.
In a health and care context, experience rating is most likely to be used for group insurances. The
past experience of the scheme in question will play a key part in determining the premium for the
next year.
For individual business, no claims discounts (which are a specific type of experience rating) may
be used for PMI (and possibly also IP insurance).
         the initial derivation of premiums for a company new to this line of business.
Rates thereafter will be revised and refined as its own experience emerges and as market
information indicates relevant trends.
In some cases (eg fully guaranteed IP policies ceasing at retirement) this process may require the
actuary to make long‐term judgements about each parameter in the assumption set. In other
cases (eg reviewable CI contracts and particularly PMI) the assessment is over a much shorter
period, typically between one and five years.
The assumptions that should be used for pricing health and care insurance products are
discussed in the following two chapters.
If the market became more competitive, margins in the pricing basis would need to be reduced.
Eliminating negative reserve values is prudent, however, in this case, management wants the
reserves to be calculated on a best‐estimate basis.
This method will generally be used when management wishes to have a best estimate of the
company’s financial performance. This might arise in circumstances where the insurer is to
be sold or where directors wish to reward key staff for their specific contributions to the
overall growth of the company. Regulatory reporting might also require base reserves to be
calculated on a best estimate basis.
Part of the uncertainty that contributes to the risk margin (risk premium) in the pricing basis is
due to the uncertainty of future volume and mix of sales. This uncertainty does not exist when
considering existing business.
Sickness data: The main risk relates to the setting of claim inception and recovery rates for the overseas market.
There are various sources of data that the insurance company might draw on, but all of these
have weaknesses. For example, the insurance company’s data from its own in‐force policies could
be of some use but the overseas market experience may differ because of different:
         general health of population                                                                        [¼]
         underwriting levels and approach                                                                    [¼]
         target market                                                                                       [¼]
         distribution method and channels                                                                    [¼]
         provision by the State                                                                              [¼]
         attitudes towards work and claiming benefits.                                                       [¼]
The insurance company might be able to use industry data (if available) …                                     [½]
… or data from the overseas country through reinsurers (or consultancies).                                    [½]
These would also need careful adjustment because:                                                     [½]
         these data might be very heterogeneous, reflecting a wide range of different policy
conditions, underwriting practices and target markets                                       [½]
         the different contract design (unit‐linked versus without‐profits) may attract a different
cross‐section of customers.                                                               [½]
Some use might be made of any national statistics on illness or on sickness absence in the
overseas country.                                                                                     [½]
These would have to be treated with great caution as a guide to an insured population.                [½]
Any of these data might also be of poor quality in themselves, for example there may be missing
or inaccurate data.                                                                           [½]
Although the insurance company is a significant player in its own market and so its data ought to
be good quality.                                                                                [½]
Some data may be too far out‐of‐date, or there may not be sufficient data for reliable results. [½]
Expense data: The company would also usually use its own data as a basis for its expense assumptions.               [½]
However, these may be entirely unsuitable for the new market …                                        [½]
… because of different operating practices, regulation, distribution methods and commission
Also, since unit‐linked sickness contracts in the overseas market are rare, there may be additional
expenses needed to educate sellers / consumers about the design.                                 [½]
It should be possible to get some information from the new market, but significant judgement will
Persistency data: A similar problem exists for persistency. Any domestic data may be almost irrelevant to the
… where selling practices and customer behaviour may be quite different.                              [½]
Any information from the overseas market might also be misleading, particularly given that they
relate to a completely different design of contract.                                          [½]
Overall: The main overall problem resulting from all the above is that it is very difficult to price the
contract with confidence. This means that the insurance company is at risk of making a loss on
The insurance company may therefore need to price with significant margins, or rely on variable
charges to correct things if they start to go wrong.                                          [½]
Syllabus objectives: 4.4       Discuss the determination of supervisory reserves and solvency capital
requirements for a health and care insurance company.
4.4.1      Describe the purposes of reserves, solvency capital requirements and
embedded value, and the methodologies by which they are calculated for a
 role of statistical and indivi:          setting assumptions, including a comparison with those used in
 Value‐at‐Risk (VaR) capital as: (The items in bold are covered – or partly covered – in this chapter.)
Syllabus objectives continued: 5.1     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for health and care insurance business.
5.1.1     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for pricing health and care
5.1.2     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for determining liabilities.
5.1.3     Explain why the assumptions used for supervisory reserves may be different
5.1.4     Outline the principles of setting assumptions for determining embedded
0 Introduction: These four chapters on assumptions deal with the construction of a basis. By basis, we mean the
set of assumptions (sickness rates, expenses, interest rates etc) that would be used for a
particular modelling application, such as pricing, or projecting the future solvency position of the
This chapter covers some general topics associated with setting assumptions.
It will start by discussing the considerations when setting assumptions for any purpose, and then
will go on to highlight the differences when setting assumptions for specific purposes. The Core
Reading specifically covers three different purposes – pricing, reserving and ongoing profitability
measurement. However, other modelling purposes demand generally similar approaches to
assumption setting, and you should aim to be able to describe the principles involved for any of
Chapters 14 and 15 cover demographic and financial assumptions respectively. Chapter 16 covers
By the end of the final assumptions chapter you should expect to understand how the actuary
goes about deciding on a suitable assumption for each parameter. The key part of the process is
the determination of what future experience is expected. A second aspect is what other factors
may need consideration: for example, the extent to which margins against adverse future
experience are required. What these other factors are will depend greatly on the purpose of the
1.1 General considerations when settin: The principles behind setting assumptions are similar to those for any actuarial work;
         consider the use to which the assumptions will be put – for example, is it to price or
cost a new financial contract or transaction, or to estimate the provisions that need
to be held to meet future liabilities?
         take particular care over the choice of the assumptions that will have the most
 consider the needs of the clie:          ensure that the parameters derived from data are produced as accurately as the
         ensure that the data used to derive these assumptions are relevant to the risks that
         ensure that the parameters are produced accurately from the data
         ensure that the data used to derive assumptions are relevant to the insured lives
         ensure that bases used are flexible to reflect changing risk circumstances.
By ‘risks’ here, the Core Reading means:          ensure that bases used for periodic valuations and reserves are flexible to reflect
The assumptions made should be responsive to any changes in the circumstances of the
in‐force policyholders that might affect the experience of the company.
Failure to adhere to these principles increases the potential for downside risk to the insurer.
The need for a credible database that is relevant to the risks to be insured and from which
assumptions can be drawn, is fundamental to the process, both for pricing and valuation.
We will see how these considerations affect our choice of assumptions as we go through the
1.2 General process for setting assump: Deriving assumptions for future experience follows a common framework.
1.        Investigate the historical experience and make best estimates of the parameters from that
2.        Consider what the conditions (including the commercial and economic environment) will
be like in the future period for which the assumptions will apply to.
3.        Determine what the best estimates of the assumptions will be, given the expected future
4.        The best estimates may need to be adjusted in order to include a margin for prudence.
The size of any margin incorporated into an assumption will depend on the purpose for
which the model is required, and the degree of risk associated with the parameter.
The assumptions used will be discussed under various headings in the next three chapters.
Question: List the assumptions that might need to be set for a health and care insurer.
For what sort of business is experience rating most likely to be used?
If the level of each of the above factors is increased, how would you expect to adjust the margins
in your pricing basis?
Why would there be no elimination of negative values when setting best estimate reserves?
Under what other circumstances might a best estimate of financial performance be appropriate?
 CI claim incidence rates:          LTCI claim inception rates and any further transition probabilities
Sources will be suggested but no attempt will be made to suggest sample figures, which for
health and care insurance products, will vary substantially around the world.
For example, you won’t be required to recommend a suitable claim incidence rate basis for CI
1.3 Consistency: One of the guiding principles in creating a valid basis is the principle of consistency. The
assumptions need to be considered in their totality, not in isolation.
Consistency means ensuring that realistic allowance is made for the way that variables behave
together, where correlations exist between them; indeed, sometimes the relationship between
two parameters will be more important than the absolute values of those parameters. (This is
similar to the idea of including dynamic links between variables in models.)
 book-rated (rather than experi: Experience rating is the practice by which the premium depends wholly or partially on the
past experience of the policyholder. Where the past experience of the policyholder is not
taken into account, book rates are used.
2.2 Basis: The assumptions we will derive in Chapters 14, 15 and 16 will be estimates of the expected
In other words, the assumptions are best estimates of future experience.
However, it may be necessary to include a margin (or margins) in the basis in order to allow for
the risk of adverse experience. The basis actually chosen for pricing, inclusive of margins, will fix
the level of risk the insurer will be subject to once the product is issued at that price.
2.3 Margins needed: Where uncertainty is prevalent in future outcomes, the prudent actuary creates margins to
ensure, as far as possible, that the insurer’s promises are met. This is especially the case
in health and care insurance. The volume of relevant statistics is not nearly as credible as
in life insurance; claim outcomes are often more subjective (less clear-cut) and more
influenced by factors outside the control of the insurer eg social and economic
circumstances. It is fair to say that the ‘funnel of doubt’ is wider for health and care
insurance than it is for pure life insurance cover.
The extent of the margins depends heavily on the purpose of the basis concerned. If the purpose
is pricing, then the margins will also be influenced by the need to have competitive premiums.
Nevertheless, the margins must reflect the risks involved, and hence the size of the margins must
 the financial significance of: All sources of risk and the extent of these sources of risk should therefore be brought to a
consideration of the margins required for pricing (or, in general, for other purposes for which a
basis is required). Chapters 23 to 25 will cover risk.
Where a cashflow model is being used to price an insurance contract, the risk to the
company from adverse future experience may be allowed for through the:
Applying margins to the expected values: At one extreme, all margins could be incorporated in the assumptions for each individual
parameter and the risk discount rate would then (in theory) be the risk‐free rate.
If a formula model, as described in earlier subjects (eg Subject CM1) and in Chapter 11, were
being used for pricing, the last two approaches above are not available and the risk of
adverse future experience would be allowed for by taking margins.
However, such a model does not help actuaries to quantify what these margins might be
and so they must use judgement based on past experience.
Using a stochastic approach: Modelling will play a big part in assessing the margins, as the actuary will employ stochastic
processes to view the likely outcomes of variables with differing distributions.
There are also useful deterministic modelling exercises that the actuary can do in this direction, as
Assuming a higher discount rate: Instead of applying margins to expected values, best estimates for the individual parameters
might be used, and margins for risk included by assuming a higher risk discount rate.
These alternatives can lead to the same result (in effect, the same premium), but they are
different ways of expressing the margin and the resulting risk. In each case, the risk of loss occurs
once the margin is ‘used up’, whether that be the margin in the individual parameter assumption,
or the risk premium in the risk discount rate.
The risk discount rate will be discussed: Note that the same principles will apply whether the margins are applied to the risk premium in
the risk discount rate (which would be the normal approach when pricing), or through adjustment
to the individual parameter assumptions (which is more likely when reserving).
2.4 The ultimate price for the contrac: Although the primary worry is that of adverse experience compared with assumptions, it is not
the only consideration: marketability and competitiveness are also important. For example, when
determining the incidence rate for pricing a CI insurance contract:
         if the incidence rate is under‐estimated the insurer is at risk of a substantial loss
         if the incidence rate is over‐estimated the insurer is at risk of not selling the product.
However, it is worse to under‐estimate morbidity than to over‐estimate it. It is better to end up
selling low volumes of very profitable business than it is to sell high volumes of loss‐making
If an insurer is selling badly rated business it may sell a lot of it before realising it is loss making,
whereas if its rates are too high, it will soon realise this (from observation of competitors’ rates)
(So in this case, you will need margins to guard against the risk that the incidence rate is
under‐estimated, eg assume incidence rates that are higher than best estimates.)
The price ultimately charged, and the margins incorporated, will depend on several factors
 company’s USP (unique selling: A company may be said to have a high USP if it is the only company in a market selling a
particular product. The lower the company’s USP, the more similar its products are to
If the company became more risk averse,: If the credibility, accuracy, relevance and ‘up‐to‐date‐ness’ of the data improved, the margins
If the size of the company’s free assets or parental guarantee increased, margins could be
3.1 Introduction: Reserving is covered in detail in Chapters 21 and 22. It is sufficient here to understand that an
insurer must carry out a valuation to demonstrate solvency to the regulatory authorities and to
use in preparing its published financial results. It may also perform an ‘internal’ valuation, which
will serve to give a more realistic idea of the exact position of the insurer than does the
3.2 General principles: The starting point for reserving assumptions will be the expected future experience.
The principles involved in deriving these assumptions will be equivalent to those used in
deriving assumptions for pricing purposes – assuming that premiums are costed on a best
Broadly, long-term insurance liabilities can be determined as the present value of future
claims plus expenses (including commission) plus taxes (if appropriate), less premiums.
It is then necessary to apply margins to these expected values so that the probability that
the resulting reserves will prove to be inadequate is below an acceptably low figure. What
is acceptably low will depend upon the reason for valuing the contracts and any relevant
The greater the margins that are employed in the assumptions, the more prudent is the
It should be noted that, all else being equal, decreasing the discount rate increases the
degree of prudence for a positive reserve (the opposite being the case for a negative
The immediate question in building a suitable basis for determining reserves is the purpose of the
Published valuations tend to be carried:          for reporting company results, ie the ‘published accounts’ (such as would be of interest to
The published accounts: The assumptions used to determine the liabilities to be shown in an insurer’s published
accounts should be consistent with the legislation and accounting principles governing the
preparation of those accounts in the territory concerned.
The following should be considered:          whether the accounts are to be prepared on a going concern basis or a break-up
         whether the accounts are required to show a true and fair view
         whether reserves are required to be assessed as best estimates or on some other
basis, and precisely how the terms used are to be interpreted.
The solvency accounts: If separate accounts are required as part of the process of supervision of solvency, the
rules governing the preparation of those separate accounts may or may not be the same as
those that apply to the published accounts.
They may, for example, be required to be prepared on a going concern basis or on a
break-up basis.
The accounts could also have to be prepared on a run‐off basis. This means assuming the
insurance company would continue to service in‐force business but would cease to write any new
business. This contrasts with a break‐up basis that assumes the insurance company is wound up
and policies with unexpired periods of cover are cancelled (probably with a proportion of the
premium returned to the policyholder).
Reference should be made to those rules and any guidance that may have been issued as to
their interpretation.
There will almost certainly be some constraints on the assumption decisions for the purpose of
preparing the solvency accounts. These constraints might be:
         specific, eg the valuation interest rate must equal the premium basis interest rate
         more general, eg the insurer may do the valuation as it wishes, subject to the final result
not being lower than it would have been under a prescribed regulatory basis.
In some countries (including those in the European Union), there has been movement
towards a supervisory regime under which the base reserves are calculated on a best
estimate or market-consistent basis, with additional risk margins held (see Chapter 22).
So there are two main approaches to sett:          assumptions that include a margin for prudence, eg a low valuation interest rate
         best estimate assumptions with a separate allowance for risk, eg a risk margin or
Whichever of these approaches is used, the actuary should ensure that the overall level of
reserves and solvency capital is prudent.
Methods of calculating reserves and solvency capital are discussed in Chapter 22.
3.3 Valuing liabilities on a best esti: Under different circumstances, the actuary will be called upon to provide ‘best estimates’ of
Here assumptions will be derived based on the actuary’s best estimate of future realistic
experience, without the incorporation of any prudential margins. The basis derived will be
similar to that used for new business pricing.
A cashflow method would estimate, possibly case-by-case or possibly with some grouping,
the amounts that need to be held now to meet future claims (including options), expense
and tax outgo against the expected premium and investment revenue inflow.
In other words, the cashflow method projects future income and outgo, and the present value of
any shortfall would be the reserves needed to be held now.
All experience items will be allowed for explicitly (expenses, persistency, investment return,
There would generally be no elimination of any negative reserves (unless regulations did
not permit these to be held).
When setting reserves on a policy‐by‐policy basis, the calculated reserves on some policies might
be negative, eg if the expected future premiums are more than sufficient to cover expected future
claims and expenses. If elimination of negative values applies, then these negative reserves
The internal management accounts: The principles to be followed in preparing internal management accounts are matters to be
discussed and agreed with the insurer. The aim is likely to be to produce expected values
of the future experience, based on realistic assumptions.
The managers will want to see a realistic picture of the financial state of the company that is not
distorted by including margins in the assumptions. Internal management accounts could be
prepared for just about any purpose, for example to look at:
3.4 Statutory reserving vs pricing ass: In some countries it is standard practice to calculate premium rates using prudent
assumptions and then to use the same assumptions to calculate the reserves for
In other countries, it is standard practice to calculate premiums using assumptions that
broadly reflect expected future experience, with the risks to the company being allowed for
mainly through the risk discount rate. If the supervisory regime in that country requires
prudence in reserving assumptions, then it would not be appropriate for the same
assumptions to be used both for pricing and for reserving.
So, if the same assumptions are used for reserving as were used in pricing, then the reserves
would not be prudent enough for supervisory purposes.
However, as noted above, some regimes have been moving towards the use of best
estimate assumptions in reserves. In that case, the underlying basis could be the same for
both pricing and reserving, but additional allowances for risk would be needed for each.
4     Deriving assumptions for determining the profitability of existing
4.1 Introduction: The assumptions required for determining the profitability of existing business are
The principles involved here are the same as those that apply to deriving assumptions for
pricing purposes using a cashflow model. However, this does not necessarily mean that
the values for the parameters will be the same, in respect of a contract, for both purposes.
In particular, the allowances made for r: In addition, the timeframe will be slightly different. For pricing work, the insurer often wants
assumptions that will be valid as from any start point over a non‐trivial period of time (eg the next
year or the next three years), so that it can avoid having to reprice every month. However, for
work on profitability, it can apply the most appropriate values from the start point of now.
Regarding the allowances made for risk, the risk discount rate will probably be lower than in the
Measuring profitability: The profitability of existing business is often measured by the embedded value of the insurer.
The calculation of EV will actually requ:          a reserving basis – regardless of the purpose for which the EV is being calculated, the
supervisory reserving basis would always be used; it is needed in order to calculate:
–          the net assets (ie the assets less the current reserves)
–          the change in reserves in the future, which is part of the calculation of the present
         a projection basis – the insurer may wish to use a different projection basis depending on
the purpose for which the EV is being calculated; it is needed in order to project the
insurer’s experience into the future (another part of the calculation of the present value
It is the projection basis that is referred to in the remainder of this section.
The value of the business as a whole, both the existing business and the future new business, is
Both embedded value and appraisal value are discussed in more detail in Chapter 21.
4.2 Assumptions: The key determinant in deciding on an EV basis is the purpose for which the EV is being
If an embedded value is being prepared for published accounts or internal management
accounts, then the principles to be applied in setting the assumptions are as outlined in
The calculation of EV for internal management accounts is likely to be carried out on a best
estimate basis. Given that the purpose of calculating an EV is to give a more realistic value of the
insurer, then a best estimate basis is likely to be used for published accounts also, although
regulations may require the use of a more prudent basis.
EV will be measured regularly (for example, quarterly) to see how the company is progressing,
ie how the management is increasing the company’s worth to shareholders. If possible, the EV
will be broken down into distribution channels, or subsidiaries, to measure their progress, and so
also the extent to which their management is contributing to the company.
Projected EVs will be used as a criterion in assessing any financial or strategic management
decisions – for example, the choice between two strategies will be heavily weighted in favour of
the one that generates the higher projected EV.
If an appraisal value is being prepared as a sale value, then it is likely to be based on
realistic assumptions without margins. A purchase value may be based on cautious
assumptions that include margins.
As a result, a purchaser is likely to offer a lower price for the company than the seller thinks it is
worth. The final price agreed for the business will then depend on the relative bargaining power
of the buyer and seller.
For embedded value financing, the reinsurer will also perhaps be restrained in deciding the
amount of future ceding company cashflows that can be factored to a current commission
payment.
Embedded value financing involves the reinsurer making a loan to the insurer which is repaid
from future profits. If the future profits do not emerge as expected then the reinsurer will not be
paid. So the reinsurer is likely to perform this calculation using prudent assumptions.
These types of arrangement are discussed: The determination of bonuses for staff and salespeople could warrant a realistic or perhaps
slightly prudent basis. The actuary will need to be mindful of the greater unpredictability of
health and care insurance business than of life insurance.
The insurer will not want to pay bonuses to its staff now for business that turns out to be
unprofitable in the future. So a degree of prudence might be included in the basis to reduce the
Some insurers possess a small health and care portfolio in relation to their total business.
These insurers will align their assumptions to the bulk of their in-force business from the
other areas of the insurance business in force.
4.3 Allowing for risk: Embedded value calculations need to include an appropriate risk margin to allow for the
unpredictability of profit emergence for health and care insurance business.
The purpose of an EV is to give a more realistic assessment of the value of the insurance company
to its shareholders. A best estimate of the profit in the tenth year might be 100, but this will not
be worth as much to the shareholders as a risk‐free cashflow of 100 in the tenth year. So the EV
needs to be reduced in some way to reflect the risks of the business.
The rate of interest used to discount the future streams of surplus will traditionally reflect
the risk inherent in these amounts. It should be noted that, all else being equal, increasing
the discount rate increases the degree of prudence.
So, one possible way of allowing for risk is to use a risk discount rate that is higher than the
risk‐free rate. The more risky the future profits, the higher the risk discount rate should be.
A stochastic or market-consistent approa: A stochastic projection would highlight the range of possible values that future profits might take.
Under a market‐consistent approach the discount rate used would be the risk‐free rate. The
investments would be assumed to earn the risk‐free rate (regardless of whether or not risk‐free
assets are held). A risk margin would be deducted from the EV to reflect non‐investment risks,
eg by using the cost of capital approach that is described in Chapter 22.
Subject to the other considerations noted above, the EV experience assumptions may be
the same as those used in pricing.

Deriving assumptions for pricing purpose: The starting point is a best estimate basis for the assumptions, however, margins will be
necessary to guard against adverse future experience.
Where a cashflow model is being used, the risk from adverse future experience may be
 the company’s attitude to risk:          the credibility, accuracy, relevance and ‘up‐to‐date‐ness’ of the data
         the size of the company’s free assets or parental guarantee.
Deriving assumptions for reserving purpo: Again, the starting point is a best estimate basis for the assumptions.
For some purposes – including the published accounts and the statutory solvency accounts –
margins may be incorporated. Consideration will need to be given to whether the accounts
are being produced on a going concern or break‐up basis, whether they are required to give a
true and fair view and whether there is any legislation or guidance on the basis or
For other purposes – including the internal management accounts – margins may not be
It is common in some countries to price prudently and then to define the reserving basis as
the pricing basis. In other countries, premiums are calculated using a realistic basis, with
risks to the insurer being allowed for through the risk discount rate assumption.
Deriving assumptions for determining pro: The basis used is likely to be fairly realistic, although it will depend on the specific purpose.
